theme: The Realm of Poetry and Discovery
text:
Imagine a winter evening when a silent room fills with the loud crackle of a fire, and you decide to meet an old book again. Poetry, more than any other realm of writing, can recover what daily life misses; it lets us extract meaning from the ordinary and anticipate the joy of understanding. A valuable verse is real in a way that gold is not, for its worth does not depend on currency or financial markets.

When I was barely sixteen, in my teens, a strict teacher who would preside over our class with marked dignity asked us to mend our lazy habits and read conscientiously. He spoke toward us as a specialist might address a junior colleague. "Several of you," he said, "treat learning as rubbish to be tossed aside, yet the synthesis of fact and feeling is what gives a mind its height." His words, given freely, achieved more than any threat. Immediately I felt a vigorous hunger to know more.

That hunger took me far. I read of the universe and the galaxy, of radioactivity and the slow propagation of light across space; of how a flask in a lab might hold a combustible gas, of how scientists measure the tonnage of a ship or chart the song of whales. I learned that to amend a theory is no shame, that to abridge a long work can clarify it, and that a dubious claim should never be embraced without proof. A good reader, like a good farmer who tends his wheat and fowl, must not forsake patience.

There is a kind of admiration that borders on reverence for those who, against starvation and penury, still create. A warrior may win glory in battle, a giant of industry may amass wealth, but the poet who writes through bitterness leaves something that will not blur. History gives us the madman and the realist alike; it shows the explosive force of rebellion and the slow extraction of justice from injustice. To preside over one's own thoughts, to pertain to no faction blindly, to perceive clearly: this is the manifestation of a free mind.

Now, recovered from a long illness, I sit by the dike near my home and watch the water disperse the evening light. My cheek feels the cold; an ear catches a distant infection of laughter from children at play. I think of an antique flask of perfume my mother kept, of a sausage supper after a hurried day, of the juicy pear I shared with a friend. Marketing and expenditure will always demand their share of our hours, and a dispute over money can sour any week. But scarcely a day passes that I do not, clockwise around the room, let my eyes fall on the books and feel, despite every hardship, indefinite and quiet gratitude. My perception of these matters did not come in a hurry. As a folk saying goes, you cannot legislate taste, nor disburse wisdom like coins. When a postponement notice from the printer arrived, I felt a jolt run from hip to bowel, for the despatch of my favourite volume had been delayed. To achieve equality of access, the library waived the tuition of its renovation classes, and an obese cat would smack its lips and climb onto the old chair we had begun to upholster anew. I would not miss those evenings: the radioactive glow of the lamp, a pronoun underlined in red, the worn hull of a paperback, a soft nightgown, and the smack of rain on the glass.
translation:
想象一个冬夜，寂静的房间里充满了炉火响亮的噼啪声，而你决定再次与一本旧书相会。诗歌，比任何其他写作领域都更能找回日常生活所遗漏的东西；它让我们从平凡中提取意义，并预期理解的喜悦。一首有价值的诗以黄金所不具备的方式真实存在，因为它的价值不取决于货币或金融市场。

当我刚满十六岁、还在青少年时期时，一位会以鲜明的尊严主持我们班级的严格老师，要求我们改正懒散的习惯、认真地读书。他朝我们说话的样子，就像一位专家对一位较年轻的同事讲话。"你们中有几个人，"他说，"把学习当作可以丢弃的垃圾，然而事实与情感的综合才是赋予头脑高度的东西。"他慷慨给予的话语，比任何威胁都更有效。我立刻感到一种强烈的求知渴望。

那种渴望带我走得很远。我读到宇宙和星系，读到放射性和光在空间中缓慢的传播；读到实验室里的烧瓶可能装着一种易燃气体，读到科学家如何测量一艘船的吨位或绘制鲸的歌声。我懂得了修正一个理论并不可耻，删节一部长篇作品能使它更清晰，而一个可疑的论断绝不应在没有证据的情况下被接受。一个好读者，就像一个照料小麦和家禽的好农夫，不能放弃耐心。

有一种近乎崇敬的钦佩，献给那些在饥饿与贫困中仍然创作的人。战士可以在战场上赢得荣耀，工业巨头可以积累财富，但那个在辛酸中写作的诗人留下的东西不会模糊。历史既给我们疯子也给我们现实主义者；它展示反抗的爆炸性力量，以及从不公中缓慢提取正义的过程。主宰自己的思想，不盲目附属于任何派别，清晰地感知：这就是自由心灵的表现。

如今，从一场久病中痊愈，我坐在家附近的堤旁，看水面把傍晚的光线驱散开。我的脸颊感到寒冷；一只耳朵捕捉到远处孩子们玩耍时传染般的笑声。我想起母亲保存的一瓶古董香水，想起匆忙一天后的香肠晚餐，想起我和朋友分享的多汁的梨。营销和开支永远会索取我们时间的份额，一场关于金钱的争执能让任何一周变得糟糕。但几乎没有哪一天，我不会顺时针环视房间，让目光落在书上，并感到——尽管有种种艰辛——一种不确定而宁静的感激。 我对这些事的领悟并非匆忙得来。正如一句民间谚语所说，你无法用立法规定品味，也无法像分发硬币那样支付智慧。当印刷厂的延期通知送达时，我感到一阵震颤从髋部直贯肠腹，因为我最爱的那卷书的发送被推迟了。为实现取阅的平等，图书馆免除了其翻新课程的学费，一只肥胖的猫咂着嘴，爬上我们重新开始装饰的旧椅子。我不愿错过那些夜晚：灯下放射性般的光晕、用红线划出的一个代词、平装书磨损的外壳、一件柔软的睡衣，以及雨点打在玻璃上的啪嗒声。
